content:
# Luke Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 113-114 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_020_page_001.jpg, LUK_020_page_002.jpg

---

**1** One day, while Jesus was teaching the people in the temple and proclaiming the good news, the chief priests and the scribes came up to him, along with the elders, **2** and confronted him: "Tell us — by what authority are you doing these things? Who gave you this authority?" **3** He answered them, "I also will ask you a question. Tell me: **4** Was John's baptism from heaven, or was it merely a human invention?" **5** They discussed it among themselves, saying, "If we say, 'From heaven,' he will ask, 'Then why did you not believe him?' **6** But if we say, 'A human invention,' all the people will stone us, because they are convinced that John was a prophet." **7** So they answered that they did not know where it came from. **8** Jesus said to them, "Then neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things."

**9** He then began to tell the people this parable: "A man planted a vineyard, leased it out to tenant farmers, and went away for a long time. **10** When the time came, he sent a servant to the tenants so they would give him his share of the vineyard's fruit. But the tenants beat the servant and sent him away empty-handed. **11** So he sent another servant. They beat that one too, treated him shamefully, and sent him away with nothing. **12** He sent yet a third, and they wounded this one as well and threw him out.

**13** "Then the owner of the vineyard said, 'What shall I do? I will send my beloved son. Perhaps they will respect him.' **14** But when the tenants saw him, they discussed it among themselves and said, 'This is the heir. Let us kill him, so that the inheritance will be ours.' **15** So they threw him out of the vineyard and killed him. What then will the owner of the vineyard do to them? **16** He will come and destroy those tenants and give the vineyard to others."

When the people heard this, they said, "May this never happen!" **17** But Jesus looked directly at them and said, "Then what does this scripture mean: 'The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone'? **18** Everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces, and it will crush anyone on whom it falls."

**19** The scribes and chief priests wanted to seize him at that very moment, because they realized he had told this parable against them — but they were afraid of the people.

**20** So they watched for an opportunity and sent spies who pretended to be sincere, hoping to catch him saying something they could use to hand him over to the authority and jurisdiction of the governor. **21** These spies asked him, "Teacher, we know that you speak and teach what is right, and that you show no favoritism but truly teach the way of God. **22** Is it lawful for us to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?"

**23** But Jesus saw through their deception and said to them, **24** "Show me a denarius. Whose image and inscription are on it?" They said, "Caesar's." **25** He said to them, "Then give back to Caesar what belongs to Caesar, and give to God what belongs to God." **26** They were unable to trap him with anything he said in front of the people. Amazed by his answer, they fell silent.

**27** Some of the Sadducees — who deny that there is a resurrection — came to him with a question. **28** "Teacher," they said, "Moses wrote for us that if a man's brother dies, leaving a wife but no children, the man should marry the widow and raise up offspring for his brother. **29** Now there were seven brothers. The first one married a woman and died without children. **30** The second married her, **31** and then the third, and in the same way all seven died without leaving any children. **32** Finally, the woman died too. **33** So in the resurrection, whose wife will she be? For all seven had married her."

**34** Jesus replied, "The people of this present age marry and are given in marriage. **35** But those who are considered worthy of taking part in the coming age and in the resurrection from the dead will neither marry nor be given in marriage. **36** In fact, they cannot die anymore, because they are like the angels. They are children of God, being children of the resurrection. **37** But that the dead are raised — even Moses made this clear at the burning bush, where he calls the Lord 'the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' **38** He is not the God of the dead, but of the living, for to him all are alive."

**39** Some of the scribes responded, "Teacher, that was well said." **40** And after that, no one dared to ask him anything more.

**41** Then Jesus said to them, "How can people say that the Messiah is the son of David? **42** For David himself says in the Book of Psalms: 'The Lord said to my Lord, sit at my right hand **43** until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.' **44** David calls him 'Lord' — so how can he be David's son?"

**45** While all the people were listening, he said to his disciples, **46** "Beware of the scribes. They love to walk around in flowing robes, and they love being greeted with honor in the marketplaces. They want the most important seats in the synagogues and the places of honor at banquets. **47** They devour the estates of widows and make a show of saying long prayers. These men will receive the most severe punishment."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **20:1-2 — The challenge to Jesus' authority**: The confrontation is initiated by a coalition: HOI ARCHIEREIS KAI HOI GRAMMATEIS SUN TOIS PRESBYTEROIS (hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis sun tois presbyterois, "the chief priests and the scribes together with the elders"). This is essentially the Sanhedrin — the full governing council of Judaism — demanding Jesus' credentials. Their question — EN POIA EXOUSIA TAUTA POIEIS (en poia exousia tauta poieis, "by what authority are you doing these things") — refers not only to his teaching but to the temple cleansing of 19:45-46. In their world, authority was delegated through recognized channels: ordination, appointment, dynastic succession. Jesus holds none of these credentials. The word EXOUSIA ("authority") will dominate this chapter, as every subsequent encounter tests whether Jesus has the right to speak and act as he does.

- **20:3-8 — The counter-question about John's baptism**: Jesus' response is not evasion but a masterful exposure of his opponents' intellectual dishonesty. He poses a genuine dilemma: TO BAPTISMA IŌANNOU EX OURANOU ĒN Ē EX ANTHRŌPŌN (to baptisma Iōannou ex ouranou ēn ē ex anthrōpōn, "was John's baptism from heaven or from humans?"). The leaders' private deliberation reveals their calculation: they are not seeking truth but managing public opinion. They fear the crowd will KATALITHASEI (katalithasei, "stone") them — a term for mob execution — if they deny John's prophetic status. Their answer OUK OIDAMEN (ouk oidamen, "we do not know") is not humble ignorance but strategic refusal. Jesus' reply matches theirs: those who refuse to exercise honest judgment forfeit the right to demand it from others.

- **20:9-12 — The parable of the wicked tenants**: The vineyard imagery is unmistakable to anyone steeped in the prophets: Isaiah 5:1-7 established the vineyard as a metaphor for Israel under God's care. The owner (God) plants, entrusts, and departs — APEDĒMĒSEN CHRONOUS HIKANOUS (apedēmēsen chronous hikanous, "went away for a considerable time"). The sent servants represent the prophets, and the escalating violence against them — DEIRANTES (deirantes, "having beaten"), ATIMASANTES (atimasantes, "having dishonored"), TRAUMATISANTES (traumatisantes, "having wounded") — mirrors Israel's historical pattern of rejecting God's messengers (cf. 2 Chronicles 36:15-16, Nehemiah 9:26). Each sending represents another act of divine patience; each rejection represents a deepening of human defiance.

- **20:13-16 — The sending of the beloved son**: The owner's deliberation — TI POIĒSŌ; PEMPSŌ TON HUION MOU TON AGAPĒTON (ti poiēsō; pempsō ton huion mou ton agapēton, "what shall I do? I will send my beloved son") — reveals not naivety but the exhaustion of every other option. The phrase HUION MOU TON AGAPĒTON ("my beloved son") echoes the divine voice at Jesus' baptism (3:22) and transfiguration (9:35), making the allegorical identification unmistakable. The tenants' reasoning — APOKTEINŌMEN AUTON HINA HĒMŌN GENĒTAI HĒ KLĒRONOMIA (apokteinōmen auton hina hēmōn genētai hē klēronomia, "let us kill him so that the inheritance may become ours") — exposes the logic of all religious power that eliminates God's rightful claim in order to possess what belongs to him. That they throw the son EXŌTĒS TOU AMPELŌNOS (exō tou ampelōnos, "outside the vineyard") before killing him prefigures Jesus' crucifixion outside the city walls (Hebrews 13:12).

- **20:16-18 — "May this never happen!" and the cornerstone**: The crowd's reaction — MĒ GENOITO (mē genoito, "may it never be!") — is a horrified exclamation; they grasp the parable's implication even if they resist it. Jesus responds by quoting Psalm 118:22: LITHON HON APEDOKIMASAN HOI OIKODOMOUNTES HOUTOS EGENĒTHĒ EIS KEPHALĒN GŌNIAS (lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes houtos egenēthē eis kephalēn gōnias, "the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone"). The KEPHALĒ GŌNIAS ("head of the corner") was the crucial stone that locked two walls together and bore the weight of the structure. Verse 18 adds a double warning: PAS HO PESŌN EP' EKEINON TON LITHON SYNTHLASTHĒSETAI (pas ho pesōn ep' ekeinon ton lithon synthlasthēsetai, "everyone who falls on that stone will be shattered"), and EPH' HON D' AN PESĒ LIKMĒSEI AUTON (eph' hon d' an pesē likmēsei auton, "on whomever it falls, it will crush him to dust"). The verb LIKMAŌ means to "winnow" or "scatter like chaff" — total pulverization. The stone cannot be ignored: one either stumbles over it or is crushed by it.

- **20:19-20 — Spies sent to entrap Jesus**: The leaders' response is precisely what the parable predicted: they want to seize the son. The phrase EPHOBĒTHĒSAN TON LAON (ephobēthēsan ton laon, "they feared the people") shows the same political calculation exposed in the baptism question. They resort to ENKATHĒTOUS (enkathētous, "planted agents" or "spies") — the word literally means "those sent in to sit among," suggesting infiltrators posing as ordinary listeners. These agents HUPOKRINOMENOUS HEAUTOUS DIKAIOUS EINAI (hupokrinomenous heautous dikaious einai, "pretending to be righteous") — the verb HUPOKRINOMAI is the root of "hypocrisy," originally a theater term for playing a role. Their goal is to deliver Jesus to TĒ ARCHĒ KAI TĒ EXOUSIA TOU HĒGEMONOS (tē archē kai tē exousia tou hēgemonos, "the rule and authority of the governor") — that is, to Roman jurisdiction, where the charge would be political sedition rather than religious disagreement.

- **20:21-26 — The question about tribute to Caesar**: The spies' flattery is carefully crafted: they acknowledge Jesus does not LAMBANEIS PROSŌPON (lambaneis prosōpon, "show partiality" — literally "receive face"), hoping this reputation for fearless honesty will lead him into a politically fatal statement. The question — EXESTIN HĒMAS KAISARI PHORON DOUNAI Ē OU (exestin hēmas Kaisari phoron dounai ē ou, "is it lawful for us to pay tribute to Caesar or not?") — is a perfect trap. If Jesus says yes, he alienates the crowds who resent Roman taxation; if he says no, he commits treason. Jesus' request to see a DĒNARION (dēnarion, "denarius") — a Roman silver coin bearing Tiberius's image and the inscription TIBERIUSCAESARDIVI AUGUSTIFILIUS ("Tiberius Caesar, son of the divine Augustus") — turns the trap inside out. The coin itself testifies to Caesar's claim; its very presence in their pockets shows they already participate in Caesar's economy. His answer — APODOTE TA KAISAROS KAISARI KAI TA TOU THEOU TŌ THEŌ (apodote ta Kaisaros Kaisari kai ta tou theou tō theō, "give back to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's") — uses APODOTE ("give back," "render"), implying not a gift but a return of what already belongs to each party. The deeper question — what bears God's image and therefore belongs to God? — is left unspoken but unmistakable: human beings, created in God's image (Genesis 1:27), owe themselves entirely to God. Caesar may claim his coins; God claims his creatures.

- **20:27-33 — The Sadducees' question about resurrection**: The Sadducees (HOI ANTILEGONTES ANASTASIN MĒ EINAI, hoi antilegontes anastasin mē einai, "those who deny that there is a resurrection") accepted only the Torah (the five books of Moses) as authoritative and found no explicit resurrection teaching there. Their hypothetical scenario is based on the law of levirate marriage (EPIGAMBREUSĒ, from Deuteronomy 25:5-6): if a man dies childless, his brother must marry the widow to produce an heir. By constructing an absurd scenario of seven successive husbands, they aim to show that resurrection leads to logical impossibility. The question TINOS AUTŌN GINETAI GUNĒ (tinos autōn ginetai gunē, "whose wife does she become?") assumes that resurrection simply restores earthly conditions — a assumption Jesus will demolish.

- **20:34-36 — Jesus' answer: the nature of resurrection life**: Jesus distinguishes between HOI HUIOI TOU AIŌNOS TOUTOU (hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou, "the children of this age") and HOI KATAXIŌTHENTES TOU AIŌNOS EKEINOU TYCHEIN (hoi kataxiōthentes tou aiōnos ekeinou tychein, "those considered worthy to attain that age"). Marriage belongs to this age because it serves the propagation of mortal life. In the resurrection, OUTE GAMOUSIN OUTE GAMIZONTAI (oute gamousin oute gamizontai, "they neither marry nor are given in marriage") — not because resurrection diminishes human experience but because it transcends the conditions that make marriage necessary. The resurrected are ISAGGELOI (isaggeloi, "equal to angels") — a word found only here in the New Testament — not in the sense of becoming angelic beings but in the specific sense of being beyond death and therefore beyond the need for procreation. They are HUIOI THEOU (huioi theou, "children of God") precisely because they are TĒS ANASTASEŌS HUIOI (tēs anastaseōs huioi, "children of the resurrection") — their identity is defined by the new reality God creates, not by the old categories of mortal existence.

- **20:37-38 — Moses and the burning bush**: Having demolished the Sadducees' assumption about the nature of resurrection, Jesus now proves its reality from their own accepted scripture. At the burning bush (Exodus 3:6), Moses calls the Lord TON THEON ABRAAM KAI THEON ISAAK KAI THEON IAKŌB (ton theon Abraam kai theon Isaak kai theon Iakōb, "the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob"). The argument turns on the present tense of the divine self-identification: God does not say "I was" their God but "I am" their God. A God who defines himself in relationship to the dead would be, as it were, the God of nothing. The conclusion is devastating to Sadducean theology: THEOS OUK ESTIN NEKRŌN ALLA ZŌNTŌN PANTES GAR AUTŌ ZŌSIN (theos ouk estin nekrōn alla zōntōn pantes gar autō zōsin, "he is not the God of the dead but of the living, for to him all are alive"). The phrase PANTES GAR AUTŌ ZŌSIN ("for all are alive to him") is remarkable — it asserts that Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are currently alive in God's sight, not merely awaiting a future resurrection. Death does not sever the covenant bond between God and his people.

- **20:39-40 — Silence of the questioners**: The scribes' response — DIDASKALE KALŌS EIPAS (didaskale kalōs eipas, "Teacher, you have spoken well") — is striking because the scribes were Pharisees who believed in the resurrection. Jesus has just demolished their rivals' position using the Sadducees' own scriptures. Their admiration is genuine but also marks the end of public challenges: OUKETI ETOLMŌN EPERŌTAN AUTON OUDEN (ouketi etolmōn eperōtan auton ouden, "they no longer dared to ask him anything"). Every trap has been turned back on its setter; every question has exposed the questioner rather than the questioned.

- **20:41-44 — David's Lord**: Having answered all challengers, Jesus now poses his own question: PŌS LEGOUSIN TON CHRISTON EINAI DAUEID HUION (pōs legousin ton Christon einai Daueid huion, "how can they say that the Messiah is David's son?"). He quotes Psalm 110:1 — EIPEN KYRIOS TŌ KYRIŌ MOU KATHOU EK DEXIŌN MOU (eipen kyrios tō kyriō mou kathou ek dexiōn mou, "the Lord said to my Lord, sit at my right hand"). The argument is simple but devastating: if David, writing under the Spirit's inspiration, calls the Messiah KYRIOS ("Lord"), then the Messiah cannot be merely David's descendant. A father does not call his son "Lord." The Messiah must be something greater than a restored Davidic king — he must be one who shares God's throne. Jesus does not deny Davidic descent (which Luke has carefully established in chapters 1-2) but insists it is radically insufficient as a category. The Messiah is not less than David's son, but incomprehensibly more.

- **20:45-47 — Warning about the scribes**: Jesus' final words in this chapter are a public denunciation delivered AKOUONTOS PANTOS TOU LAOU (akouontos pantos tou laou, "while all the people were listening"). The scribes love STOLAIS (stolais, "long robes") — the flowing garments that marked religious distinction. They crave ASPAMOUS EN TAIS AGORAIS (asmapous en tais agorais, "greetings in the marketplaces") — the elaborate public salutations that conferred honor. They seek PRŌTOKATHEDRIAS EN TAIS SYNAGŌGAIS (prōtokathedrias en tais synagōgais, "the chief seats in the synagogues") and PRŌTOKLISIAS EN TOIS DEIPNOIS (prōtoklisias en tois deipnois, "the places of honor at banquets"). Each detail describes religion performed for social prestige. The most damning charge is last: HOI KATESTHIONTES TAS OIKIAS TŌN CHĒRŌN (hoi katesthiontes tas oikias tōn chērōn, "who devour the houses of widows"). The verb KATESTHIŌ ("devour" or "eat up") suggests predatory consumption — these religious leaders exploit the very people they are supposed to protect. Their PROPHASEI MAKRA PROSEUCHOMENOI (prophasei makra proseuchomenoi, "making a show of praying at length") exposes prayer itself as a tool of exploitation: the long prayers build a reputation for piety that gives them access to vulnerable households. The sentence — HOUTOI LĒMPSONTAI PERISSOTERON KRIMA (houtoi lēmpsontai perissoteron krima, "these will receive greater condemnation") — warns that religious privilege intensifies rather than mitigates judgment. The warning sets up the story that opens chapter 21: the widow who gives her last two coins, the very kind of person these scribes exploit.

- **20:1-47 — The chapter as a whole**: Luke 20 is a sustained contest over authority. Every encounter is an attempt to either establish or undermine Jesus' right to teach, act, and claim what he claims. The chief priests ask, "By what authority?" The spies test whether he will acknowledge Caesar's authority or defy it. The Sadducees challenge his teaching authority on resurrection. And Jesus himself raises the ultimate question: what kind of authority does the Messiah hold? The chapter reveals a consistent pattern: those who challenge Jesus are exposed by their own questions. The religious leaders cannot answer the baptism question because they have never honestly engaged it. The spies are silenced by a coin they carry in their own pockets. The Sadducees are refuted from the very scriptures they claim to honor. And in the end, no one dares ask anything more — not because Jesus has silenced inquiry, but because every insincere question has ricocheted back on the questioner. The chapter moves from challenged authority to demonstrated authority to transcendent authority: Jesus is not merely a teacher with clever answers but the Lord whom David himself acknowledged. The warning about the scribes then serves as a final indictment: those who hold religious authority but use it for self-enrichment and exploitation will face the harshest judgment of all. Authority in God's kingdom is measured not by the honor it accumulates but by the vulnerable people it serves.
